text: As we advance the camera into the chest, we're looking at the right lung with the diaphragm located on the left, and we see that there are an abundance of adhesions and plaques from the previous talc pleurodesis procedures.

The adhesions are lysed and then the right lung is picked up to reveal a collection of serious fluid underneath it that fluid is suctioned up and then blunt dissection begins to try to locate the thoracic duct leakage.
And this is when the spy imaging is used for the first time toggling between normal vision infrared vision and spy gain.

You can see that it's not very helpful to see normal infrared, but when you overlay it with augmented reality, you can see the fluorescent imaging on top of what you would normally see with a thoracoscopic camera.

We attempt blunt dissection to try and localize it, but when fluid starts to leak out of the left side of the field, we go and check behind the posterior portion of the right lung to identify the source of the leakage.
Before we can do that though, the spy is turned off because its brightness makes it difficult to see and effectively operate in the field.
Before going around to the posterior portion of the lung, a large thick adhesion is identified that is clipped and cut with a harmonic scalpel.
The adhesion was clipped because we were unsure if there was an unnamed vessel behind it and wanted to minimize any potential bleeding.
Once the adhesion is cut, we attempt some blunt dissection behind it to try and locate the leakage.
Confident that this is not the source of it, we apply one final clip and move on to look at the posterior of the right lung where we see that there's a collection of fluorescent lymphatic fluid.
This is the first time that we directly visualize lymphatic fluid being ejected from the thoracic duct lesion into the chest.
it's suctioned up and the spy gain is reduced to determine exactly where it's coming from.
Pressure is applied directly to the thoracic duct using the clamp to see if any additional fluid can be ejected.
And you can see that it comes directly out of that opening.
Once the spy gain is turned off, you can really appreciate that it's nearly impossible to distinguish the fluorescent lymphatic fluid, which becomes clear,
from the serous fluid and blood that may be in the field.
At this point, we begin suturing the visceral pleura of the lung to the parietal pleura of the chest wall in an attempt to close the tissue on top of itself and prevent additional leakage.
As it's tied down, the two edges of the opening begin to approximate, and without fluorescent imaging, it would be impossible to tell if it was still leaking.
As the spygain is turned back on, it becomes apparent that there's still leakage and that additional sutures are required.
Additional sutures are applied in the same fashion with the visceral pleura of the lung being sutured directly to the parietal pleura of the chest wall.

While it might have been standard to perform a ligation in mass of the thoracic duct, we were unable to do so in this case because of fibrosis and inflammation secondary to the previous talcopluridices.
As the pleura is being sutured, we see that ICG clearly distinguishes fluorescent lymphatic fluid from serosanguinous fluid.
This is another opportunity to highlight that with any other modality, this would not be possible, reinforcing the utility of intraoperative ICG usage in lymphatic visualization.
As this next set of sutures is tied down, we can see that there's still leakage coming out of the thoracic duct opening.
It's suctioned away and pressure is again applied to the duct to see how much fluid is being ejected.
As more sutures are applied, direct pressure to the thoracic duct or lung tissue produces less and less lymphatic fluid being ejected.
As this final portion of suture is tied down and cut, you can appreciate that there's no additional leakage out of the thoracic duct opening.
This direct visualization and the fact that no additional leakage was seen even with direct pressure from the suction arm makes us confident that the leak was closed up.
A layer of bio-glue sealant was applied in the hopes that any residual leakage would be trapped underneath it.
The spygain is turned up to maximum intensity to make sure that there's no additional leakage.
A final talc pleurodesis is performed with the hopes that fibrosis and inflammation would prevent any additional leakage in the future.
